summary: The verse 'which is for himself' resolves R. Elazar's dilemma by establishing exclusive designation: the Yom Kippur service covers only its specific atonement. The HP walks west through the Sanctuary on the right (south) side as a mark of kavod. Inside the Holy of Holies, he sprinkles the bull's blood once upward and seven times downward using the counting formula 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' which anchors every count with 'one' to prevent losing track.

display.whats: Does 'which is for himself' resolve R. Elazar's question about personal obligations in Yom Kippur service?
display.hint: The verse 'which is for himself' (asher lo) provides the textual basis for exclusive designation: the Yom Kippur service covers only its specific atonement, not any personal obligations.
display.title: 'Which Is for Himself': The Verse That Closes the Door on Dual Intent

learning.ahaMoment: A single phrase 'which is for himself' locks an entire service to its designated purpose - no personal intent may attach to it.
learning.memoryAnchor: 'Which is for himself' - the verse seals the Yom Kippur service to its designated atonement only.
learning.learnerQuestion: Where does the Gemara find textual support for R. Elazar's ruling that the Yom Kippur service cannot serve as a personal vow fulfillment?
learning.coreTension: R. Elazar's ruling requires a textual anchor, since the prohibition against using the designated service for a personal vow is not stated outright but must be derived from the verse's specific language.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reads 'asher lo' ('which is for himself') as a limiting phrase that restricts the Yom Kippur bull's purpose exclusively to the HP's own designated atoning role, excluding any additional personal-vow function - the limiting phrase seals the exclusivity ruling.

takeaway.text: The phrase 'asher lo' ('which is for himself') provides the textual basis for R. Elazar's ruling: it limits the Yom Kippur bull's purpose exclusively to the HP's designated atoning role, excluding any co-opting for a personal vow.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may read 'asher lo' as an expansive phrase permitting the HP personal benefit from the service, missing that the Gemara reads it as a limiting phrase that closes off any additional personal-vow function.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'Which is for himself' means the Yom Kippur service is designated for its specific atonement - personal obligations cannot be discharged through it.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: 'Which is for himself' means the Yom

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Elazar's question is answered: the phrase 'which is for himself' proves exclusive designation.
argumentFlow.label: Resolution: R. Elazar's question is answered: the phrase

halachic: 'which is for himself' establishes exclusive designation
Yom Kippur service cannot discharge personal obligations

misconceptions.misconception: 'For himself' (asher lo) means the High Priest may personally benefit from and direct the Yom Kippur service, including using it to fulfill a personal vow.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara reads 'asher lo' as a limiting phrase that restricts the bull's purpose exclusively to the HP's designated atoning role - it excludes, rather than expands, any personal-vow use.

visualizableElements.description: 'Which is for himself' - a single phrase that locks service to its designated purpose

quizSeeds.answer: The verse pins the Yom Kippur service to its specific designated atonement. 'For himself' means this service covers its designated purpose only - not personal votive obligations the HP may have.
quizSeeds.question: How does 'which is for himself' resolve R. Elazar's question?

sugyot.finalRuling: The verse 'which is for himself' establishes that the Yom Kippur service is exclusively designated for its specific atonement.

display.whats: The HP walks west through the Sanctuary on the right (south) side as a mark of respect
display.hint: The HP enters from the east and walks toward the Holy of Holies heading west. The rule is to walk on the right side - a general principle of showing kavod (respect) in entering important spaces.
display.title: Entering on the Right: The High Priest's Path Through the Sanctuary

learning.ahaMoment: Entering any important space: turn right as a mark of respect. In the Temple, this convention takes on supreme significance.
learning.memoryAnchor: HP enters heading west, walks south side (right) - kavod in the holiest space on the holiest day.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the High Priest enter the Sanctuary on the right (south) side rather than any other path?
learning.coreTension: The HP's choice of side when entering the Sanctuary might seem like a personal or incidental convention, but the Gemara grounds the right-side path in a halachic norm of respect rather than custom or preference.
learning.coreMove: Turning right upon entry is a halachic norm of respect (kavod) applied in the Temple as a legal requirement, not a convention the HP may vary - his path through the Sanctuary on the right side is governed by this norm.

takeaway.text: The High Priest enters the Sanctuary on the right (south) side because turning right upon entry is a halachic norm of respect - it is a legal requirement in the Temple, not a matter of personal custom.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the HP's right-side path is a personal or customary choice with no legal significance, missing that the Gemara grounds it in a halachic respect norm applied as a Temple requirement.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: The HP walks through the Sanctuary on the right (south) side heading west toward the Holy of Holies.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The HP walks through the Sanctuary on

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Walking on the right when entering is a mark of kavod (respect); the HP shows maximum deference in the Temple.
argumentFlow.label: Explanation: Walking on the right when entering is

halachic: HP walks south side toward Holy of Holies

misconceptions.misconception: The HP's path through the Sanctuary on the right side is a matter of personal custom or preference.
misconceptions.correction: Turning right upon entry is a halachic norm of respect, applied in the Temple as a legal requirement. The HP's path follows the general rule: turn right when entering any important space.

quizSeeds.answer: Walking on the right side is a mark of respect (kavod). The HP demonstrates maximum deference to the sacred space by following this directional convention when entering.
quizSeeds.question: Why does the HP walk on the right side of the Sanctuary?

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP walks on the right (south) side of the Sanctuary heading west - a mark of respect (kavod).

display.whats: Blood sprinkling inside the Holy of Holies: one upward, seven downward, with counting formula 'achat, achat v'achat...'
display.hint: The HP sprinkles the bull's blood on the Ark cover: once upward then seven times downward, using the counting formula 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' to track each sprinkle without losing count.
display.title: Once Up, Seven Down: The Counting Formula for Blood Sprinkling

learning.ahaMoment: The counting formula was engineered to make losing track nearly impossible - anchoring every count with the initial 'one' is brilliant mnemonic design for the most critical moment of the year.
learning.memoryAnchor: Sprinkling formula: 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' - the initial 'one' anchors every subsequent count so the HP cannot lose his place.
learning.learnerQuestion: How does the High Priest count the blood sprinklings inside the Holy of Holies, and in which direction does each sprinkling go?
learning.coreTension: The blood must be sprinkled once upward and seven times downward, but the order and counting formula are precise - inverting the direction or losing count would invalidate the service, making the exact formula essential.
learning.coreMove: The Mishna establishes the formula: one sprinkling upward, then seven downward, counted as 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' to ensure the HP maintains count even inside the Holy of Holies where no assistant is present.

takeaway.text: The High Priest sprinkles the blood once upward and then seven times downward, counting 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' - the one upward sprinkling comes first, not last.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may invert the formula, imagining seven upward sprinklings followed by one downward, missing that the single upward sprinkling precedes all seven of the downward ones.

argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: HP sprinkles: once upward, then seven times downward, counting 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...'
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP sprinkles: once upward, then seven times

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The formula keeps 'one' (achat) in every count so the HP can always identify his position in the sequence.
argumentFlow.label: Explanation: The formula keeps 'one' (achat) in every

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The counting must prevent any possibility of error during the most sacred and high-stakes moment of the service.
argumentFlow.label: Principle: The counting must prevent any possibility of

halachic: one upward seven downward sprinkling sequence
counting formula prevents error and restart

misconceptions.misconception: The High Priest sprinkles the blood seven times in an upward motion and once downward inside the Holy of Holies.
misconceptions.correction: The formula is the reverse: one sprinkling upward first, then seven times downward, counted with the formula 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' to maintain an accurate count.

quizSeeds.answer: The HP counts 'achat, achat v'achat, achat ushtayim...' - keeping 'one' in every count anchors his position. If he loses count he must restart. The formula was designed to make losing track nearly impossible during the most sacred moment of the year.
quizSeeds.question: What is the sprinkling counting formula and why was it invented?

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP sprinkles once upward and seven times downward using the anchoring counting formula.

rashiTranslations.en: 'Which is for himself' - the verse designates the Yom Kippur service exclusively for its specified atonement.
This phrase resolves R. Elazar's dilemma: the HP cannot use this service for personal votive obligations.
The designated atonement is the bull's blood for himself and his household - nothing additional may be incorporated.
R. Elazar's question is answered: the Yom Kippur service is legally exclusive in both directions.
The HP enters the Heikhal (Sanctuary) and walks on the south side - the right side when facing west.
Walking right when entering from the east toward the west is conventional respect (kavod).
The HP proceeds westward toward the parochet (curtain) leading to the Holy of Holies.
He carries the blood bowl in his right hand and walks respectfully toward the innermost sanctum.
The general principle: when entering an important space, turn right as a sign of honor.
This applies in the Temple and analogously in other contexts requiring respect.
The Gemara confirms: this is a kavod convention, not a separate halakhic requirement for the Temple.
The HP demonstrates maximum respect because he is about to enter the most sacred space in existence.
The HP stands before the parochet and enters the Holy of Holies.
He sprinkles the blood: once upward toward the kapporet, then seven times downward below it.
The upward sprinkle is above the ark cover; the seven are directed below it.
The counting formula: achat (one), achat v'achat (one-and-one), achat ushtayim (one-and-two)...
Each count includes the initial 'one' (achat) to anchor the HP's position in the sequence.
The formula prevents error: including 'one' in every count means the HP always knows his place.
After 'achat v'sheva' (one-and-seven), the bull blood sprinkling is complete: eight total (one up plus seven down).
The HP then exits to receive the goat blood for the next sprinkling sequence.
The counting formula is the Sages' error-prevention mechanism for the most critical service act.
No other Temple service has a dedicated counting formula - this one does because of the stakes.
If the HP loses count he must restart the entire sprinkling sequence from the beginning.
The formula means he can only lose track of a single sprinkle at most - the 'one' anchor is always present.
This section completes the description of the bull blood sprinkling inside the Holy of Holies.
The subsequent goat blood sprinkling follows the identical formula.
The two sprinklings together - bull plus goat - provide atonement for different categories of sin.
The bull blood atones for the HP and his household; the goat blood atones for all Israel.
Together they cover the full scope of Yom Kippur atonement.
The next section (52a) describes the HP's exit and return for the incense service.
The sprinkling direction (one up, seven down) is Mishna-level halacha.
The counting formula ('achat, achat v'achat') is also Mishna-level - the prescribed method.
Any deviation in count or direction invalidates that sprinkle and requires repetition.
The HP must complete all eight sprinkles correctly; partial completion is insufficient.
The Holy of Holies sprinkling is complete only when all eight are done without error.
The HP then walks out backward - he does not turn his back to the Ark location.
Exiting backward is discussed on 53a.
Summary of 51b: 'asher lo' verse, right-side entry, and the sprinkling formula.
All three establish the precision and exclusivity of the HP's innermost service.
Rashi here is unusually detailed - this passage describes the most sacred act in Temple service.
Every word of the Mishna is precise and every word of Rashi is necessary for understanding it.
The HP's experience inside the Holy of Holies was unique in human history.
The Gemara's attention to detail reflects the gravity of this singular moment.
Rashi's commentary here is among his most important contributions to understanding Yom Kippur.
The 'one upward, seven downward' sequence appears throughout the blood services of Yom Kippur.
The Holy of Holies uses this sequence for both the bull and the goat blood.
The outer sprinkling (before the parochet) uses a similar sequence described later in the tractate.
Rashi connects these sequences to show the systematic structure of the entire Yom Kippur blood service.
The Yom Kippur service is one of the most systematically structured religious acts ever described.
Each act follows the previous in a legally mandated sequence - deviation requires correction.
This systematic structure reflects that Yom Kippur atonement requires total precision.
The HP is not performing a symbolic ritual - he is executing a legally mandated God-prescribed sequence.
End of 51b Rashi; the next entries on 52a deal with the HP's return for the incense service.
Summary of 51b Rashi: designated-service exclusivity, kavod in entering, sprinkling technique and formula.
These three topics together describe the complete inner-service procedure from entry to sprinkling completion.
The 51b section is the heart of the Yom Kippur service description in tractate Yoma.
Its legal, procedural, and spiritual significance cannot be overstated.
Rashi's commentary here is indispensable for understanding what actually happened inside the Holy of Holies.
End of 51b Rashi section.
The 52a section begins with the HP's entry protocol and one-cubit partition discussion.
